skill.name: readme-for-developers
skill.description: Create developer-focused README files that help engineers understand project goals, architecture, setup, operations, and working agreements.
skill.summary: Build onboarding README documentation for software teams.
skill.author: Doyajin174

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced a high score by treating Markdown fences, inline README examples, placeholder URLs, and sample secret names as executable behavior. Manual review found no prompt injection, no credential exfiltration, and no malicious network behavior. The remaining risk is low because the skill is allowed to read, edit, write, and run Bash commands while preparing documentation.

low_findings.title: Broad File and Bash Tool Access
low_findings.description: The skill manifest allows Read, Glob, Grep, Edit, Write, and Bash. This access is appropriate for README generation but can inspect and modify workspace files, so users should run it only in trusted repositories.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools line explicitly grants file and shell capabilities. The skill intent is documentation work, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

low_findings.title: Repository Inspection Commands
low_findings.description: The workflow suggests running find and cat commands to inspect folders, package metadata, and .env.example. These commands support documentation but may expose project structure or configuration names to the active AI session.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are present in a Bash example block and align with README discovery. They do not send data externally, but they do read local project information.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Static False Positives
low_findings.description: The many Ruby backtick execution alerts are Markdown code fences and inline README examples, not executable Ruby code. No evidence found of dynamic code execution in the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are inside Markdown fences or inline Markdown formatting. The repository contains only SKILL.md, and no Ruby execution context is present.

low_findings.title: Placeholder Secret and URL Static False Positives
low_findings.description: The DATABASE_URL, .env.local, SECURITY.md, and hardcoded URL alerts occur in a sample README template or reference list. They document expected README content and do not access secrets or transmit data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited strings are clearly placeholders, local documentation links, or public documentation references. No network call or secret-reading instruction accompanies them.

content.value_statement: New engineers often lose time because README files omit setup, architecture, and operational details. This skill creates a practical README structure for onboarding, local development, debugging, and team workflow.
actual_capabilities: Defines an eleven-section README structure for developer onboarding.
Covers project goals, architecture, core flows, repository map, and local setup.
Includes templates for configuration, secrets, data models, CI, release, and debugging.
Provides a quality checklist for validating whether new engineers can use the README.
Guides repository inspection with simple commands before drafting documentation.
limitations: It does not automatically verify that generated commands work in every project.
It relies on available repository files and may need human input for missing architecture details.
It is optimized for software engineering READMEs, not marketing pages or end-user manuals.
It may require review before documenting private deployment or security procedures.

use_cases.title: Onboard New Engineers
use_cases.description: Create a README that explains setup, core flows, debugging points, and working agreements for new team members.

use_cases.title: Document a New Repository
use_cases.description: Turn a sparse project into a structured technical entry point with architecture, commands, and release notes.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team Handoffs
use_cases.description: Use a consistent README format when transferring ownership of services, tools, or internal applications.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic README
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a new project README.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a developer-focused README for this repository. Include project purpose, local setup, common commands, and a short repo map.

prompt_templates.title: Improve an Existing README
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the current README and improve it for new developer onboarding. Keep useful content, add missing setup steps, and organize it with clear sections.

prompt_templates.title: Add Architecture and Core Flows
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze the repository and update the README with system goals, architecture overview, two core flows, important entry points, and debugging guidance.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Full Handoff README
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request for team transfer or service ownership.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a complete handoff README using the developer onboarding structure. Include configuration, secrets policy, data model, CI/CD, release, rollback, observability, security, working agreement, and docs index.

output_examples.input: A repository has source code but no useful README.
output_examples.output: A structured README with a fast path, project purpose, setup steps, command table, repo map, and links to deeper documentation.

output_examples.input: A README explains installation but omits architecture and operations.
output_examples.output: An updated README that adds core flows, architecture notes, debugging points, CI behavior, deployment notes, and rollback guidance.

output_examples.input: A team is handing over a service to another team.
output_examples.output: A handoff-oriented README that records ownership context, working agreements, security expectations, and operational references.

best_practices: Keep the README as the entry point and link deeper details to docs files.
Include copy-ready setup commands and explain required environment variables.
Validate the README against a new engineer checklist before publishing it.
anti_patterns: Writing marketing language without setup or architecture details.
Listing folders without explaining their role in the system.
Putting every detail in the README instead of linking to deeper documentation.

faq.question: What kind of README does this skill create?
faq.answer: It creates developer onboarding READMEs that explain setup, architecture, workflows, debugging, and team practices.

faq.question: Can it update an existing README?
faq.answer: Yes. It can reorganize existing content and add missing sections when repository context is available.

faq.question: Does it write marketing copy?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on technical handoff documentation for engineers.

faq.question: Does it inspect project files?
faq.answer: It may inspect files such as package metadata, folder structure, and example environment files to document the project accurately.

faq.question: Is it safe for private repositories?
faq.answer: It can be used in private repositories, but users should review generated content before publishing sensitive operational details.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
